enrichment.short_description: NordPass is a cloud-based password manager from Nord Security that stores passwords, passkeys, and payment details in an encrypted vault with autofill, breach monitoring, and cross-device sync.
enrichment.company_size: Part of Nord Security (1,000+ employees group-wide)
enrichment.seo_title: NordPass Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: NordPass review 2026: pricing (from $1.38/mo), encryption, autofill, passkeys, and how it compares to other password managers. See if it fits your needs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: NordPass is a password manager and digital vault developed by Nord Security, the same Lithuanian-founded, Panama-headquartered company behind NordVPN. Launched in 2017 and built by former NordVPN engineers, NordPass uses the XChaCha20 encryption algorithm (a step beyond the AES-256 standard used by many competitors) to secure passwords, passkeys, credit card details, secure notes, and identity documents in a zero-knowledge vault, meaning Nord Security itself cannot read stored data. The product targets both individual consumers and businesses: personal users get a Free plan (one device, unlimited passwords) or paid Premium and Family tiers, while organizations use Teams, Business, or Enterprise plans with centralized admin controls, activity logs, and SSO/SCIM options at the top tier. Pricing is aggressive relative to category leaders like 1Password and Dashlane, with Premium starting near $1.38 to $1.99 per month on multi-year terms and Family plans (up to 6 users) starting around $2.58 to $3.69 per month. Key differentiators include native passkey support (positioning NordPass as an early mover in the password-to-passkey transition), a built-in Data Breach Scanner that checks email addresses and passwords against known leaks, a password health report that flags weak or reused credentials, and biometric unlock across desktop and mobile apps. NordPass integrates with the broader Nord Security ecosystem (NordVPN, NordLocker, NordLayer), which appeals to users already inside that product family, and it is available as browser extensions for Chrome, Firefox, Edge, Safari, and Brave, plus native apps for Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, and Android. Its primary audience is privacy-conscious consumers, families wanting shared credential vaults, and SMBs that want a lower-cost alternative to 1Password Business or Bitwarden Teams without sacrificing enterprise features like provisioning and audit trails. Competitors include 1Password, Bitwarden, Dashlane, Keeper, and LastPass. Because NordPass is a relatively young standalone product but backed by Nord Security's larger infrastructure and security track record, it is often chosen by users who want name-brand backing at a lower price point than legacy password managers, though some enterprise buyers still prefer 1Password or Keeper for deeper third-party integrations and compliance certifications.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: NordPass is a password manager built by Nord Security, the company behind NordVPN, and was released in 2017 to give consumers and businesses a simpler way to store and autofill credentials across devices. It stores passwords, passkeys, payment cards, and secure notes in an encrypted vault protected by a single master password.

The product is positioned as a lower-cost alternative to legacy password managers while still offering enterprise-grade features such as centralized admin dashboards, activity logs, and directory integrations for larger teams. It is available as a browser extension, desktop application, and mobile app across all major platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: NordPass's core vault uses XChaCha20 encryption and a zero-knowledge architecture, meaning even Nord Security cannot access stored passwords. The Password Health feature scans a user's vault for weak, reused, or old passwords and suggests fixes, while the Data Breach Scanner checks saved emails and passwords against known leak databases.

The app supports passkey storage and autofill, letting users log in to supporting sites without a password at all. For teams, NordPass Business and Enterprise add shared item groups, granular permission controls, activity logs, and SSO/SCIM provisioning for larger organizations that need compliance-ready credential management.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: NordPass offers a permanently free plan limited to one device at a time, with unlimited password storage. Paid personal plans are Premium (single user) and Family (up to 6 users), both discounted heavily on 2-year terms compared to monthly billing.

Business pricing is split into Teams (small groups), Business (growing companies), and Enterprise (custom, with SSO and advanced provisioning), each billed per user per month with steep discounts for annual and biennial commitments.

features.title: Zero-knowledge encrypted vault
features.description: Passwords, notes, and payment details are encrypted with XChaCha20 so that only the user, not Nord Security, can decrypt the data.

features.title: Passkey support
features.description: Stores and autofills passkeys alongside traditional passwords for sites that support passwordless login.

features.title: Data Breach Scanner
features.description: Cross-references stored emails and passwords against known data breaches and alerts users to compromised credentials.

features.title: Password Health reports
features.description: Flags weak, reused, or old passwords across the vault and suggests stronger replacements.

features.title: Cross-platform autofill
features.description: Browser extensions and native apps for Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, and Android autofill logins and forms automatically.

features.title: Secure item sharing
features.description: Lets individuals and teams share specific passwords or items without exposing the full vault.

features.title: Admin console for teams
features.description: Business and Enterprise plans include centralized user management, activity logs, and permission groups.

features.title: Biometric unlock
features.description: Supports Face ID, Touch ID, and Windows Hello for quick, secure vault access on supported devices.

pros: Lower price than most major competitors, especially on multi-year plans
Zero-knowledge architecture backed by an established cybersecurity company
Native passkey support ahead of many rivals
Clean, easy-to-use interface across desktop, mobile, and browser extensions
Business and Enterprise tiers include SSO and provisioning for compliance needs
cons: Free plan restricts vault access to a single device at a time
Fewer third-party integrations than 1Password or Keeper
Multi-year billing required to reach the lowest advertised prices
Company's Panama and Nord Security corporate structure draws occasional scrutiny from privacy researchers
Advanced features like emergency access are less mature than some competitors

faqs.question: Is NordPass free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, NordPass has a permanent free plan with unlimited password storage, though it limits vault access to one device at a time.

faqs.question: Who owns NordPass?
faqs.answer: NordPass is developed by Nord Security, the cybersecurity company that also created NordVPN and NordLocker.

faqs.question: Does NordPass support passkeys?
faqs.answer: Yes, NordPass can store and autofill passkeys in addition to traditional username and password credentials.

faqs.question: How does NordPass encrypt data?
faqs.answer: NordPass uses XChaCha20 encryption with a zero-knowledge model, so passwords are encrypted and decrypted locally on the user's device.

faqs.question: Can I share passwords with family or team members?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Family plan supports up to 6 users with shared folders, and Business and Enterprise plans support item and folder sharing with granular permissions.

faqs.question: What platforms does NordPass support?
faqs.answer: NordPass has apps for Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, and Android, plus browser extensions for Chrome, Firefox, Edge, Safari, and Brave.

faqs.question: How does NordPass compare to 1Password?
faqs.answer: NordPass is generally cheaper and has native passkey support, while 1Password offers more mature third-party integrations and a longer enterprise track record.

use_cases.title: Individual password management
use_cases.description: A single user consolidates all logins, payment cards, and secure notes into one encrypted, autofill-enabled vault.

use_cases.title: Family credential sharing
use_cases.description: Families share select logins such as streaming services or shared bills across up to 6 accounts through the Family plan.

use_cases.title: Small business credential governance
use_cases.description: Growing teams centralize shared logins, enforce password health policies, and gain visibility into who accessed what through admin logs.
use_cases.audience: Small and mid-sized business IT teams

enrichment.short_description: NordVPN is a virtual private network service from Nord Security that encrypts internet traffic, masks IP addresses, and bundles threat protection and cloud storage into tiered subscription plans.
enrichment.company_size: Part of Nord Security (1,000+ employees group-wide)
enrichment.seo_title: NordVPN Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: NordVPN review 2026: pricing from $3.09/mo, server network, speed, security features, and how it compares to other leading VPN services.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: NordVPN is a virtual private network service founded in 2012 by childhood friends Tomas Okmanas and Eimantas Sabaliauskas, originally incubated inside the Lithuanian startup accelerator Tesonet before spinning out into Nord Security, which is now headquartered in Panama with additional offices in Lithuania, the UK, and the Netherlands. It is one of the highest-profile consumer VPN brands globally, competing directly with ExpressVPN, Surfshark, and Proton VPN. NordVPN encrypts a user's internet connection and routes it through one of thousands of servers across 100-plus countries, hiding the user's real IP address and enabling access to geo-restricted content, secure browsing on public Wi-Fi, and protection against ISP tracking. The company uses its own NordLynx protocol, built on WireGuard, to balance speed and security, and it maintains a strict, independently audited no-logs policy. As of 2026, NordVPN sells four tiers billed monthly, annually, or biennially: Basic (core VPN features), Plus (adds a password manager and Threat Protection Pro, an ad and malware blocker), Complete (adds 1TB of encrypted cloud storage via NordLocker), and Prime (adds identity theft protection, credit monitoring, and cyber insurance in supported regions). Multi-year plans bring the effective monthly cost down substantially, with entry pricing around $3.09-$3.49 per month and top-tier Prime plans reaching into the $12-$25 per month range depending on term length. A single subscription typically covers up to 10 devices simultaneously. NordVPN's target audience spans privacy-conscious consumers, travelers and expats needing to bypass geo-blocking, remote workers securing connections on public networks, and streaming users seeking access to region-locked content libraries. As part of the broader Nord Security portfolio alongside NordPass, NordLocker, and NordLayer (its business-focused network security product), NordVPN benefits from shared infrastructure, a large in-house security research team, and cross-sell bundling. Its valuation reached roughly $3 billion following a 2023 funding round, reflecting rapid growth in the consumer cybersecurity software category. Key differentiators versus competitors include an extensive server network, consistently strong independent speed test results, RAM-only diskless servers that wipe data on reboot, and bundled extras (password manager, cloud storage, dark web monitoring) that let NordVPN compete as an all-in-one digital security suite rather than a standalone VPN.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: NordVPN launched in 2012 as the flagship product of what later became Nord Security, growing from a small Lithuanian startup into one of the most recognized VPN brands worldwide. It encrypts a device's internet connection and routes traffic through a global server network to hide the user's IP address and location.

Beyond core VPN functionality, NordVPN has expanded into a broader digital security suite, bundling a password manager, malware and ad blocking, encrypted cloud storage, and identity protection features into its higher pricing tiers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: NordVPN operates thousands of servers across 100-plus countries and uses its proprietary NordLynx protocol, built on WireGuard, for fast, secure connections. Threat Protection Pro blocks malicious sites, trackers, and ads even when the VPN is off, and a kill switch prevents data leaks if the VPN connection drops.

Specialty servers support P2P file sharing, Onion over VPN, and double-hop encryption for extra anonymity. Higher-tier plans add a bundled password manager, 1TB of encrypted cloud storage, and, in some regions, identity theft monitoring and insurance coverage.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: NordVPN sells four tiers, Basic, Plus, Complete, and Prime, each available on monthly, 1-year, or 2-year billing, with per-month pricing dropping significantly on longer commitments. A single subscription covers multiple devices simultaneously.

Basic covers core VPN access, Plus layers in the password manager and advanced threat protection, Complete adds encrypted cloud storage, and Prime is the full bundle with identity protection features for eligible markets.

features.title: Global server network
features.description: Thousands of servers across 100-plus countries provide broad geographic coverage for bypassing geo-restrictions and reducing latency.

features.title: NordLynx protocol
features.description: A proprietary implementation built on WireGuard that balances high connection speeds with strong encryption.

features.title: Threat Protection Pro
features.description: Blocks malicious websites, trackers, and ads at the network level, even when the VPN itself is disconnected.

features.title: Kill switch
features.description: Automatically cuts internet access if the VPN connection drops, preventing accidental data exposure.

features.title: RAM-only servers
features.description: Servers run entirely on volatile memory and wipe all data on every reboot, reducing the risk of data retention.

features.title: Specialty servers
features.description: Dedicated servers for P2P file sharing, Onion over VPN, and double VPN hops for added anonymity.

features.title: Multi-device support
features.description: A single subscription can typically be used on up to 10 devices at once across desktop, mobile, and router apps.

features.title: Independently audited no-logs policy
features.description: NordVPN's no-logs claims have undergone multiple third-party security audits to verify the company does not retain user activity data.

pros: Large, consistently fast global server network
Strong track record of independent no-logs and security audits
Higher tiers bundle real extra value (password manager, cloud storage)
Works reliably with major streaming platforms
Broad multi-platform and multi-device support
cons: Cheapest advertised prices only apply on 2-year commitments
Renewal pricing after the promotional term is significantly higher
Some advanced features (identity insurance) are limited to select regions
Interface can feel cluttered once extra security modules are enabled
Business-focused network needs are better served by the separate NordLayer product

faqs.question: When was NordVPN founded?
faqs.answer: NordVPN was founded in 2012 by Tomas Okmanas and Eimantas Sabaliauskas, initially incubated within the startup accelerator Tesonet.

faqs.question: Where is NordVPN headquartered?
faqs.answer: Nord Security, NordVPN's parent company, is headquartered in Panama, with additional offices in Lithuania, the UK, and the Netherlands.

faqs.question: How much does NordVPN cost?
faqs.answer: Pricing ranges from about $3.09/month for the Basic plan on a 2-year term up to roughly $25/month for the Prime plan billed monthly.

faqs.question: Is NordVPN different from NordPass?
faqs.answer: Yes, NordVPN and NordPass are separate products both made by Nord Security; NordVPN is a virtual private network and NordPass is a password manager.

faqs.question: Does NordVPN keep logs of user activity?
faqs.answer: NordVPN states it follows a strict no-logs policy, which has been verified through multiple independent third-party audits.

faqs.question: How many devices can I use with one NordVPN subscription?
faqs.answer: A single NordVPN subscription typically supports up to 10 devices connected simultaneously.

faqs.question: Can NordVPN unblock streaming services?
faqs.answer: NordVPN maintains dedicated infrastructure optimized for accessing major streaming platforms, though reliability can vary by title and region.

use_cases.title: Secure browsing on public Wi-Fi
use_cases.description: Travelers and remote workers encrypt their connection when using airport, hotel, or cafe Wi-Fi networks.

use_cases.title: Bypassing geographic content restrictions
use_cases.description: Users connect to servers in other countries to access region-locked streaming libraries or websites.

use_cases.title: Privacy-focused everyday browsing
use_cases.description: Privacy-conscious individuals mask their IP address and block trackers and ads across all their devices.

enrichment.short_description: Northflank is a cloud-native application platform that combines CI/CD pipelines, managed databases, preview environments, and Kubernetes orchestration into a single self-service developer platform.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (Developer Sandbox); pay-as-you-go compute beyond that
enrichment.seo_title: Northflank Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Northflank review 2026: CI/CD, preview environments, and Kubernetes hosting explained, plus pricing and how it compares to Heroku, Render, and Railway.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Northflank is a cloud-native application platform founded in 2019 and headquartered in London, United Kingdom, built to give development teams a self-service way to build, deploy, and scale applications, databases, and background jobs on Kubernetes without needing deep Kubernetes expertise. The platform unifies several categories that are often handled by separate tools: built-in CI/CD pipelines that trigger from GitHub, GitLab, or Bitbucket commits; managed databases (Postgres, MySQL, MongoDB, Redis, and more); ephemeral, full-stack preview environments that spin up automatically for every pull request; job scheduling for batch and cron workloads; and GPU workload support for AI and machine learning teams. Northflank can run on its own managed cloud infrastructure or be deployed into a customer's own cloud account (BYOC) across AWS, GCP, Azure, or on-premises Kubernetes clusters, which appeals to enterprises with data residency or compliance requirements. The company has raised roughly $24.9 million in funding, including a Series A round in November 2024 from investors such as Bain Capital Ventures, Vertex Ventures, and Amaranthine, and operates with a lean team of roughly two dozen employees. Its pricing model is usage-based: a free Developer Sandbox plan lets individuals deploy small hobby projects at no cost, while production usage is billed via pay-as-you-go compute, storage, and networking consumption, with custom enterprise contracts available for high-scale or BYOC deployments. Northflank's core differentiator versus platform-as-a-service competitors like Heroku, Render, and Railway is that it exposes the full power of Kubernetes (RBAC, secrets management, SSO, audit logging, horizontal and vertical autoscaling) through a simplified developer experience, rather than abstracting Kubernetes away entirely. This makes it attractive to engineering teams that have outgrown simpler PaaS tools but do not want to hire a dedicated platform engineering team to manage raw Kubernetes clusters. Typical users include startups and scale-ups building microservices architectures, platform engineering teams standardizing internal developer platforms, and AI/ML teams that need GPU-backed compute alongside standard application hosting. Competitors span both directions of the market: simpler PaaS tools (Heroku, Render, Railway, Fly.io) on one side, and raw managed Kubernetes offerings (EKS, GKE, AKS) or internal developer platform tooling (Backstage, Qovery) on the other.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Northflank, founded in 2019 and based in London, is a cloud-native application platform that lets development teams deploy applications, databases, and jobs onto Kubernetes without managing raw cluster infrastructure directly. It positions itself between simple PaaS tools and unmanaged Kubernetes.

The platform can run on Northflank's managed cloud or be deployed into a customer's own AWS, GCP, or Azure account, giving enterprises flexibility over data residency and infrastructure ownership while keeping the same self-service developer experience.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Northflank bundles CI/CD pipelines that trigger from Git commits, full-stack preview environments that spin up automatically for every pull request, and managed databases into one control plane, removing the need to stitch together separate CI, hosting, and database tools.

For teams with heavier workloads, Northflank supports GPU-backed compute for AI and ML jobs, secrets management, role-based access control, SSO, and audit logging, giving it enterprise-readiness typically associated with larger platform engineering investments.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Northflank offers a free Developer Sandbox plan for hobby projects and small-scale testing. Beyond that, pricing is consumption-based, charging for compute, storage, and network usage as workloads scale.

Enterprise and BYOC (bring-your-own-cloud) deployments are priced on custom contracts, typically negotiated for organizations with high-scale workloads or specific compliance and data residency requirements.

features.title: Built-in CI/CD pipelines
features.description: Automatically builds and deploys from GitHub, GitLab, or Bitbucket commits without needing a separate CI tool.

features.title: Full-stack preview environments
features.description: Spins up ephemeral environments, including services, databases, and jobs, for every pull request.

features.title: Managed databases
features.description: Provisions and manages Postgres, MySQL, MongoDB, Redis, and other datastores directly on the platform.

features.title: Kubernetes-native infrastructure
features.description: Runs on Kubernetes under the hood while exposing a simplified interface, and integrates with EKS, GKE, and AKS.

features.title: GPU workload support
features.description: Supports GPU-backed compute for AI, ML training, and inference workloads alongside standard application hosting.

features.title: Bring-your-own-cloud (BYOC)
features.description: Can be deployed into a customer's own cloud account for data residency, compliance, or cost-control reasons.

features.title: Secrets management and RBAC
features.description: Provides centralized secrets storage, role-based access control, SSO, and audit logging for team environments.

features.title: Job and cron scheduling
features.description: Runs one-off and scheduled batch jobs alongside long-running services within the same platform.

pros: Combines CI/CD, hosting, and databases into a single platform, reducing tool sprawl
Exposes real Kubernetes power without requiring deep Kubernetes expertise
Preview environments simplify testing full-stack changes per pull request
BYOC support gives enterprises control over data residency and cloud spend
GPU support makes it viable for AI/ML workloads, not just web apps
cons: Usage-based pricing can be harder to predict than flat PaaS pricing
Smaller company size than larger cloud platform vendors
Steeper learning curve than minimal PaaS tools like Render or Railway
Enterprise and BYOC pricing requires a custom sales conversation
Smaller ecosystem of third-party integrations and community plugins than established clouds

faqs.question: What is Northflank used for?
faqs.answer: Northflank is a platform for deploying applications, databases, and background jobs on Kubernetes with built-in CI/CD and preview environments.

faqs.question: Is Northflank free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Northflank offers a free Developer Sandbox plan for hobby and small-scale projects; production usage is billed on a pay-as-you-go basis.

faqs.question: Can Northflank run in my own cloud account?
faqs.answer: Yes, Northflank supports bring-your-own-cloud (BYOC) deployments across AWS, GCP, Azure, and on-premises Kubernetes clusters.

faqs.question: How is Northflank different from Heroku or Render?
faqs.answer: Northflank exposes more of the underlying Kubernetes infrastructure (RBAC, autoscaling, GPU support) while still offering a simplified developer experience, compared to the more abstracted models of Heroku and Render.

faqs.question: Does Northflank support GPU workloads?
faqs.answer: Yes, Northflank supports GPU-backed compute for AI and machine learning training and inference alongside standard application hosting.

faqs.question: When was Northflank founded?
faqs.answer: Northflank was founded in 2019 and is headquartered in London, United Kingdom.

faqs.question: Who are Northflank's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Competitors include Heroku, Render, Railway, and Fly.io on the simpler PaaS side, and managed Kubernetes services like EKS, GKE, and AKS on the infrastructure side.

use_cases.title: Self-service internal developer platform
use_cases.description: Platform engineering teams give developers self-service deployment without managing raw Kubernetes clusters themselves.

use_cases.title: Automated pull request preview environments
use_cases.description: Engineering teams review full-stack changes, including database state, in an isolated environment before merging.

use_cases.title: AI and ML workload hosting
use_cases.description: Teams run GPU-backed training and inference jobs alongside their standard application infrastructure on one platform.

enrichment.short_description: Norton 360 is Gen Digital's consumer security suite bundling antivirus, a VPN, a password manager, cloud backup, and dark web monitoring into tiered annual subscription plans.
enrichment.starting_price: From $29.99 first year (Norton 360 Standard promotional pricing)
enrichment.headquarters: Tempe, Arizona, United States (dual HQ with Prague, Czech Republic)
enrichment.seo_title: Norton 360 Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Norton 360 review 2026: antivirus, VPN, password manager, and cloud backup bundled together. Compare plan tiers, pricing, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Norton 360 is the flagship consumer security suite from Gen Digital, the company formed in 2022 from the merger of NortonLifeLock and Avast, with dual headquarters in Tempe, Arizona and Prague, Czech Republic. The Norton brand traces back to 1982, when Peter Norton Computing was founded (later acquired by Symantec in 1990), and the 'Norton 360' bundled product name was introduced in 2007 to combine antivirus with additional security tools under one subscription. Unlike standalone Norton AntiVirus Plus, Norton 360 plans bundle multiple layers of protection: real-time malware and ransomware defense, a built-in no-log VPN (Secure VPN), a password manager, cloud backup for PCs (ranging from 2GB to 100GB depending on tier), a Smart Firewall, dark web monitoring, and, on higher tiers, identity theft protection and credit monitoring through LifeLock (US only). Gen Digital's newer AI-driven features include Norton Genie, an AI chat assistant that helps users identify scams, and Scam Protection tools targeting phishing, deepfake, and social-engineering threats. Norton 360 is sold in five main tiers: Norton AntiVirus Plus (entry-level, no VPN/backup), Norton 360 Standard (adds VPN and dark web monitoring for one device), Norton 360 Deluxe (covers up to 5 devices with more cloud backup), Norton 360 Premium (covers up to 10 devices with 75-100GB backup), and Norton 360 with LifeLock Select or Advantage (adds identity theft insurance and monitoring, US-focused). List prices for the first year commonly range from about $29.99 to $199.99 depending on tier and device count, but renewal prices increase substantially, often 50 to 140 percent, after the promotional first term, a pricing pattern that is a frequent point of criticism in independent reviews. Norton 360's target audience is mainstream consumers and families who want one subscription covering multiple security needs rather than assembling antivirus, VPN, and backup from separate vendors. Primary competitors include Bitdefender Total Security, McAfee Total Protection, Kaspersky Premium, and Malwarebytes. Norton's key differentiators are its decades-long brand recognition, consistently strong independent lab detection scores (AV-Test, AV-Comparatives), the LifeLock identity protection integration unique among mainstream antivirus vendors, and its Virus Protection Promise, a money-back guarantee if a Norton expert cannot remove a virus.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Norton 360 is the bundled consumer security suite from Gen Digital, the parent company formed by the 2022 merger of NortonLifeLock and Avast. The Norton brand dates back to 1982, and the '360' naming was introduced in 2007 to signal all-around protection beyond basic antivirus.

Rather than selling antivirus alone, Norton 360 combines malware protection with a VPN, password manager, cloud backup, and, on higher tiers, identity theft protection through LifeLock, positioning it as an all-in-one digital security subscription for consumers and families.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core protection includes real-time antivirus and ransomware defense, a Smart Firewall, and Norton's Secure VPN built into every 360 tier. Cloud backup capacity scales from a few gigabytes on entry plans up to 100GB on Norton 360 Premium, protecting files against ransomware and hardware failure.

Gen Digital has layered in AI-driven scam detection through Norton Genie and broader Scam Protection features that help users identify phishing attempts, fake texts, and deepfake-based scams, alongside dark web monitoring that alerts users if their personal data appears in a breach.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Norton sells five main consumer tiers, from Norton AntiVirus Plus up to Norton 360 with LifeLock Select or Advantage, priced per year and scaled by number of covered devices and cloud backup capacity.

First-year promotional pricing is significantly lower than renewal pricing, with renewal rates often increasing by 50 to 140 percent after the first term, so buyers should check the auto-renewal price before subscribing.

features.title: Real-time malware and ransomware protection
features.description: Continuously scans for viruses, ransomware, and spyware using signature-based and behavioral detection.

features.title: Built-in Secure VPN
features.description: Includes a no-log VPN bundled into every Norton 360 tier for encrypting internet traffic on public networks.

features.title: Cloud backup
features.description: Backs up files to Norton's encrypted cloud storage as protection against ransomware and hardware failure, with capacity varying by plan.

features.title: Password Manager
features.description: Stores and autofills passwords and payment information across devices at no extra cost within 360 plans.

features.title: Dark web monitoring
features.description: Scans dark web sources for the user's personal information and alerts them if data appears in a breach.

features.title: Norton Genie AI scam assistant
features.description: An AI-powered assistant that helps users evaluate suspicious messages, links, and calls for scam risk.

features.title: Smart Firewall
features.description: Monitors network traffic to and from the device to block unauthorized access attempts.

features.title: LifeLock identity theft protection
features.description: On select US plans, monitors for identity theft and provides restoration support and insurance coverage up to policy limits.

pros: Consistently strong malware detection rates in independent lab tests
All-in-one bundle covers antivirus, VPN, backup, and password management
LifeLock identity protection is a differentiator not matched by most antivirus competitors
Long-standing brand with decades of security research and threat intelligence
Virus Protection Promise offers a money-back guarantee for malware removal failures
cons: Renewal prices increase substantially after the first promotional year
Lower tiers exclude the VPN, backup, or multi-device coverage found in higher tiers
Can consume more system resources than lightweight competitors during scans
LifeLock identity protection features are largely limited to US customers
Bundle pricing can be more expensive than assembling equivalent standalone tools separately

faqs.question: What is included in Norton 360?
faqs.answer: Norton 360 bundles antivirus and ransomware protection, a Secure VPN, a password manager, cloud backup, a Smart Firewall, and dark web monitoring, with identity theft protection on select higher tiers.

faqs.question: How much does Norton 360 cost?
faqs.answer: First-year promotional pricing typically ranges from about $29.99 to $199.99 depending on the tier and number of devices, with renewal prices rising significantly after the first term.

faqs.question: Who owns Norton 360?
faqs.answer: Norton 360 is a product of Gen Digital, formed in 2022 from the merger of NortonLifeLock and Avast, with dual headquarters in Tempe, Arizona and Prague, Czech Republic.

faqs.question: Is Norton 360 different from Norton AntiVirus Plus?
faqs.answer: Yes, Norton AntiVirus Plus is the entry-level standalone antivirus product, while Norton 360 tiers add a VPN, cloud backup, and additional features in a bundled subscription.

faqs.question: How many devices does Norton 360 cover?
faqs.answer: Coverage ranges from a single device on Norton 360 Standard up to 10 or more devices on Norton 360 Premium and LifeLock-inclusive tiers.

faqs.question: Does Norton 360 include identity theft protection?
faqs.answer: Identity theft protection through LifeLock is included on Norton 360 with LifeLock Select and Advantage plans, primarily available to US customers.

faqs.question: What are the main alternatives to Norton 360?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Bitdefender Total Security, McAfee Total Protection, Kaspersky Premium, and Malwarebytes.

use_cases.title: All-in-one home device protection
use_cases.description: A household protects multiple PCs, Macs, and mobile devices with one subscription covering antivirus, VPN, and backup.

use_cases.title: Identity theft monitoring for US consumers
use_cases.description: Individuals concerned about identity theft add LifeLock monitoring and restoration support alongside standard antivirus protection.

use_cases.title: Secure browsing on shared or public networks
use_cases.description: Remote workers and travelers use the bundled Secure VPN to encrypt traffic when connecting to unfamiliar Wi-Fi networks.

enrichment.short_description: NotePlan is a Markdown-based notes, tasks, and calendar app for macOS, iOS, and iPadOS that unifies daily planning, task management, and note-taking in a single time-based workspace.
enrichment.seo_title: NotePlan Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: NotePlan review 2026: how this Markdown notes, tasks, and calendar app works, pricing plans, and how it compares to Notion, Obsidian, and Todoist.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: NotePlan is a productivity app created in 2016 by developer Eduard Metzger in Berlin, Germany, that merges note-taking, task management, and calendar planning into a single Markdown-based workspace for Apple platforms (macOS, iOS, and iPadOS). Its core concept is time-based note organization: instead of storing notes in an arbitrary folder tree, NotePlan centers the experience around daily, weekly, monthly, quarterly, and yearly notes, letting users write journal entries, plan tasks, and reference project notes all within the flow of a calendar-driven structure. Tasks can be typed directly into any note using Markdown syntax and then scheduled onto specific days, dragged between dates, or synced with the device's native Calendar and Reminders apps, which distinguishes it from calendar-agnostic tools like Obsidian or task-only tools like Todoist. All notes are stored as plain-text Markdown files on the user's device (with optional iCloud or CloudKit sync across devices), giving users full data ownership and future-proofing rather than lock-in to a proprietary database format, a positioning similar to Obsidian's local-first philosophy but with tighter native calendar integration. NotePlan supports templates, backlinks, tags, and a plugin/scripting system for power users who want to automate recurring workflows, and it has built a loyal niche following among the productivity and personal-knowledge-management (PKM) community, particularly users of the Getting Things Done (GTD), time-blocking, and bullet-journal methodologies. As a small, independently run software company (a solo-founder to small-team operation, in contrast to venture-backed competitors), NotePlan operates on a straightforward subscription model with a Personal plan and a Business plan, both offering a 7-day free trial and full feature access across a user's devices once subscribed. Its target audience is individual knowledge workers, consultants, writers, and planners who want the flexibility of Markdown notes without giving up dedicated calendar and task-scheduling functionality. Primary competitors include Notion, Obsidian, Craft, Things 3, and Todoist, though NotePlan differentiates itself by being the rare tool that treats the calendar, not a folder or database, as the primary organizing structure for both notes and tasks.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: NotePlan was created in 2016 by Berlin-based developer Eduard Metzger to combine note-taking, task management, and calendar planning into one Markdown-based app for Apple devices. Rather than organizing notes into folders, NotePlan centers everything around daily, weekly, monthly, and yearly notes tied to the calendar.

The app stores all content as local, plain-text Markdown files, giving users full ownership of their data and the ability to edit notes in other Markdown editors if desired, while still offering iCloud sync across macOS, iOS, and iPadOS devices.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Tasks are written inline within any note using Markdown checkbox syntax and can then be scheduled to specific calendar days, dragged between dates, or referenced across multiple notes. NotePlan syncs with the native Apple Calendar and Reminders apps, so time-blocked tasks and events appear alongside externally scheduled meetings.

Power users can extend NotePlan with templates for recurring note structures, backlinks and tags for connecting related notes, and a plugin and scripting system for automating repetitive workflows, giving it flexibility closer to Obsidian than to simpler calendar or to-do apps.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: NotePlan is subscription-based with a Personal plan and a Business plan, both offering a 7-day free trial before billing begins. A subscription unlocks full functionality across all of a user's Apple devices.
There is no permanent free tier; access to core features requires an active subscription after the trial period ends.

features.title: Calendar-centric note organization
features.description: Daily, weekly, monthly, and yearly notes form the primary structure for planning and journaling, rather than folders.

features.title: Inline Markdown task management
features.description: Tasks are written directly in notes using Markdown syntax and can be scheduled, rescheduled, or moved between days.

features.title: Native Calendar and Reminders sync
features.description: Integrates with Apple's Calendar and Reminders apps so scheduled tasks and events appear in one unified view.

features.title: Local, plain-text Markdown storage
features.description: Notes are stored as Markdown files on-device with iCloud sync, avoiding lock-in to a proprietary data format.

features.title: Templates
features.description: Reusable templates speed up recurring note types such as meeting notes, daily planning pages, or project kickoffs.

features.title: Backlinks and tags
features.description: Connects related notes through backlinks and hashtags, supporting personal knowledge management workflows.

features.title: Plugins and scripting
features.description: A plugin system lets advanced users automate workflows and extend NotePlan's functionality.

pros: Unique calendar-first organization bridges notes, tasks, and scheduling
Local Markdown file storage gives users full data ownership
Deep native integration with Apple Calendar and Reminders
Flexible enough for GTD, time-blocking, and bullet-journal workflows
Lightweight, focused app built by an independent developer team
cons: Apple-only, with no native Windows, Android, or web app
No permanent free plan, only a 7-day trial
Smaller plugin and template ecosystem than Notion or Obsidian
Steeper learning curve for users unfamiliar with Markdown syntax
Small independent team means slower feature velocity than venture-backed competitors

faqs.question: What platforms does NotePlan support?
faqs.answer: NotePlan is available for macOS, iOS, and iPadOS; there is no native Windows, Android, or web version.

faqs.question: Is NotePlan free?
faqs.answer: NotePlan offers a 7-day free trial, but ongoing use requires a Personal or Business subscription.

faqs.question: How does NotePlan store notes?
faqs.answer: Notes are stored as local, plain-text Markdown files on the device, with optional iCloud sync across a user's Apple devices.

faqs.question: Does NotePlan sync with Apple Calendar?
faqs.answer: Yes, NotePlan integrates with the native Apple Calendar and Reminders apps so scheduled tasks and events appear together.

faqs.question: Who created NotePlan?
faqs.answer: NotePlan was created in 2016 by Berlin-based developer Eduard Metzger.

faqs.question: How is NotePlan different from Notion or Obsidian?
faqs.answer: NotePlan organizes notes and tasks around the calendar as the primary structure, whereas Notion uses databases and pages and Obsidian uses a folder-based note graph.

faqs.question: Can I use NotePlan for task management alone?
faqs.answer: Yes, tasks can be created and scheduled without heavy note-taking, but NotePlan is designed to combine both in one workspace.

use_cases.title: Daily time-blocked planning
use_cases.description: Individuals plan their day by writing tasks directly into the daily note and scheduling them alongside calendar events.

use_cases.title: Markdown-based journaling
use_cases.description: Writers and consultants keep a long-running journal of daily notes that stays searchable and linkable over time.

use_cases.title: GTD and project task tracking
use_cases.description: Users following Getting Things Done or bullet-journal methods track project tasks across daily, weekly, and monthly notes.

features: Full app access across Apple devices

enrichment.short_description: Notesnook is a fully open-source, end-to-end encrypted note-taking app that offers zero-knowledge privacy with notebooks, tags, and cross-platform sync as a private alternative to Evernote.
enrichment.seo_title: Notesnook Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Notesnook review 2026: open-source, end-to-end encrypted note-taking, pricing, and how it compares to Evernote, Obsidian, and Standard Notes.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Notesnook is an open-source, end-to-end encrypted note-taking application founded in 2019 in Khushab, Pakistan, and developed and funded by Streetwriters (Private) Limited. It was built explicitly as a privacy-first alternative to Evernote and OneNote, targeting users who want a full-featured notes app (notebooks, tags, rich text, embeds, attachments) without trusting a third party with unencrypted access to their data. Every note is encrypted client-side using XChaCha20-Poly1305 for encryption and Argon2 for key derivation before it ever leaves the user's device, meaning Notesnook operates on a zero-knowledge model where even the company cannot read stored notes. Unlike most commercial note apps, Notesnook is 100 percent open source, covering not just the client applications (available for Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, Android, and web) but also the self-hostable sync server, letting privacy-maximalist users run their own backend infrastructure entirely independent of Notesnook's hosted service. The company operates on a bootstrapped, subscription-funded model rather than venture capital, with a free plan covering basic encrypted note-taking, tagging, and cross-device sync, and a Pro plan (roughly $1.99 to $8.99 per month depending on billing term, commonly cited around $4.49/month on annual billing) unlocking unlimited notebooks, note version history, rich embeds, file attachments, and a secure vault for locking individual notes with a separate password or biometric. Notesnook's target audience includes privacy-conscious individuals, students, journalists, and open-source software advocates who are wary of cloud note apps that can be subpoenaed, breached, or mined for data, positioning it alongside other privacy-first tools like Standard Notes and Obsidian (with its local-first, plugin-based sync). Its key differentiators are the combination of full open-source transparency (auditable by anyone, including the sync server) with a genuinely feature-rich editor comparable to mainstream note apps, rather than the stripped-down functionality often found in other 'private' note tools. Because it is a small, bootstrapped company based in Pakistan rather than a venture-funded Silicon Valley startup, Notesnook has grown primarily through open-source community trust, GitHub visibility, and word-of-mouth within privacy and security-focused online communities rather than traditional marketing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Notesnook was founded in 2019 by Streetwriters, a small software company based in Khushab, Pakistan, to give privacy-conscious users a full-featured, end-to-end encrypted alternative to note apps like Evernote and OneNote.

The entire product, including client apps and the sync server, is open source, which lets technically inclined users audit the encryption implementation or self-host their own sync backend instead of relying on Notesnook's hosted infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Notes are encrypted client-side using XChaCha20-Poly1305 with Argon2 key derivation before syncing, so Notesnook's servers only ever store encrypted data, following a zero-knowledge model similar to privacy-first password managers.

Beyond encryption, Notesnook includes notebooks and nested organization, tags, rich text formatting with embeds and attachments, a locked vault for sensitive individual notes, and cross-platform apps for Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, Android, and the web.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Notesnook offers a free plan covering core encrypted note-taking, tagging, and sync across devices. The Pro plan unlocks unlimited notebooks, note version history, rich embeds, and vault protection, priced roughly between $1.99 and $8.99 per month depending on the billing term chosen.
As a bootstrapped, subscription-funded company, Notesnook relies on Pro subscriptions rather than venture funding or data monetization to sustain development.

features.title: Client-side end-to-end encryption
features.description: Notes are encrypted with XChaCha20-Poly1305 and Argon2 key derivation on-device before syncing, so the server never sees unencrypted content.

features.title: Fully open-source codebase
features.description: Client apps and the sync server are both open source, letting anyone audit the code or self-host their own backend.

features.title: Notebooks and tags
features.description: Organizes notes into nested notebooks and tags for flexible categorization and retrieval.

features.title: Rich text editor with embeds
features.description: Supports formatted text, file attachments, and embedded content within notes on the Pro plan.

features.title: Secure note vault
features.description: Lets users lock individual sensitive notes behind an additional password or biometric check.

features.title: Cross-platform sync
features.description: Available on Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, Android, and the web with encrypted sync across all devices.

features.title: Note version history
features.description: Pro plan retains historical versions of notes so users can restore earlier edits.

pros: Fully open source, including the self-hostable sync server
Strong zero-knowledge, client-side encryption model
Affordable Pro pricing compared to many privacy-focused competitors
Feature-rich editor rather than a stripped-down 'private notes only' tool
Available across all major desktop, mobile, and web platforms
cons: Smaller company and team size than mainstream note-taking competitors
Free plan limits notebook count and lacks version history
Smaller plugin and integration ecosystem than Evernote or Notion
Self-hosting the sync server requires technical setup
Less brand recognition outside privacy and open-source communities

faqs.question: Is Notesnook really open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, Notesnook is 100 percent open source, including both the client applications and the self-hostable sync server.

faqs.question: How does Notesnook encrypt notes?
faqs.answer: Notesnook encrypts notes client-side using XChaCha20-Poly1305 for encryption and Argon2 for key derivation before any data is synced.

faqs.question: Is Notesnook free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Notesnook has a free plan with core encrypted note-taking and sync; a Pro plan adds unlimited notebooks, version history, and vault protection.

faqs.question: Where is Notesnook based?
faqs.answer: Notesnook is developed by Streetwriters (Private) Limited, a company based in Khushab, Pakistan, and was founded in 2019.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Notesnook?
faqs.answer: Yes, because the sync server is open source, technically capable users can self-host their own backend instead of using Notesnook's hosted service.

faqs.question: How is Notesnook different from Evernote?
faqs.answer: Notesnook is built around zero-knowledge end-to-end encryption and is fully open source, whereas Evernote does not offer end-to-end encryption or open-source transparency.

faqs.question: What platforms does Notesnook support?
faqs.answer: Notesnook has apps for Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, Android, and a web app.

use_cases.title: Private personal journaling
use_cases.description: Individuals keep a daily journal or sensitive personal notes knowing that not even the provider can read the content.

use_cases.title: Student and researcher note organization
use_cases.description: Students organize class and research notes into notebooks and tags while keeping academic material encrypted and synced across devices.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted encrypted notes
use_cases.description: Technically capable users or small organizations self-host the open-source sync server for full control over where encrypted note data lives.

enrichment.short_description: Notion is an all-in-one workspace that combines notes, documents, wikis, databases, and project management into a single flexible platform, now enhanced with built-in AI features.
enrichment.seo_title: Notion Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Notion review 2026: notes, databases, wikis, and AI features explained, plus pricing plans and how it compares to Confluence, Coda, and ClickUp.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Notion is an all-in-one workspace platform founded in 2013 by Ivan Zhao and Simon Last, headquartered in San Francisco, California, that combines documents, wikis, databases, and lightweight project management into a single block-based canvas. Every piece of content in Notion, text, images, tables, and embeds, is a modular 'block' that can be freely rearranged, nested, and linked, which lets teams build anything from a simple to-do list to a full internal wiki or product roadmap without switching tools. The company has raised roughly $418 million in funding from investors including Sequoia Capital, Accel, and First Round Capital, and reached an $11 billion valuation following a secondary tender offer in January 2026, with a headcount that has grown to approximately 4,800 employees as of 2026. Notion's pricing follows a freemium model with four tiers: Free (unlimited blocks for individuals, capped guest invites and file size), Plus at $10/user/month billed annually (unlimited file uploads, extended version history, unlocked for team collaboration), Business at roughly $20/user/month billed annually (full Notion AI access including the multi-step Notion Agent, AI meeting notes, and enterprise search across connected apps), and Enterprise (custom pricing with SCIM provisioning, audit logs, unlimited page history, and advanced security controls). Notion AI, once a standalone $8/member/month add-on available on any plan, is now bundled exclusively into Business and Enterprise tiers, reflecting the company's strategic push to make AI a core differentiator rather than an optional extra. Key differentiators versus competitors include Notion's uniquely flexible block-and-database model (letting the same underlying data power multiple views like tables, boards, calendars, and galleries), a large template and integration ecosystem built by both Notion and its community, and increasingly capable AI agents that can search across connected third-party tools and complete multi-step tasks. Notion's target audience spans individual note-takers and students on the free tier, startups and small teams using it as a lightweight project management and wiki tool, and larger enterprises adopting it as a Confluence or SharePoint replacement for internal knowledge management. Primary competitors include Confluence, Coda, ClickUp, Airtable, Asana, and Obsidian, with Notion generally positioned as the most flexible but least specialized option, capable of replacing several single-purpose tools at the cost of a steeper initial setup and configuration curve for teams that want more opinionated, out-of-the-box workflows.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Notion was founded in 2013 by Ivan Zhao and Simon Last and is headquartered in San Francisco, California. It has grown into one of the most widely used all-in-one workspace tools, combining notes, wikis, databases, and project tracking into a single flexible, block-based canvas.

The company has raised roughly $418 million in venture funding and reached an $11 billion valuation in a January 2026 secondary tender offer, with headcount growing to approximately 4,800 employees as it expands its enterprise and AI product lines.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Notion's foundational concept is the block: every piece of content, from a paragraph to a database table, is a movable, nestable block that can be rearranged or linked to other pages, letting the same workspace serve as a personal notebook, team wiki, or project tracker.

Databases can be displayed as tables, boards, calendars, timelines, or galleries from the same underlying data, and Notion AI, available on Business and Enterprise plans, adds a multi-step Notion Agent, AI meeting notes, and enterprise search across connected third-party apps.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Notion offers a permanently free plan for individuals with unlimited blocks but capped guest invites and file uploads. Plus, at $10/user/month billed annually, removes those caps and adds extended version history for small team collaboration.

Business, at roughly $20/user/month billed annually, unlocks full Notion AI functionality, while Enterprise adds SCIM provisioning, audit logs, and advanced security controls on a custom-priced contract requiring a sales conversation.

features.title: Block-based editor
features.description: Every element, text, images, embeds, and databases, is a modular block that can be freely rearranged and nested within pages.

features.title: Flexible databases
features.description: The same underlying data can be displayed as tables, boards, calendars, timelines, or galleries, adapting to different workflows.

features.title: Notion AI and Notion Agent
features.description: Business and Enterprise plans include AI-assisted writing, summarization, and a multi-step Notion Agent that can complete tasks autonomously.

features.title: Team wikis and knowledge bases
features.description: Teams build searchable internal wikis with linked pages, permissions, and version history.

features.title: Templates and integrations
features.description: A large library of first-party and community templates, plus integrations with tools like Slack, Google Drive, and GitHub, speeds up setup.

features.title: Enterprise search
features.description: Business and Enterprise tiers can search across connected third-party apps directly from within Notion.

features.title: Guest and permission controls
features.description: Granular sharing settings let teams invite external collaborators or guests with scoped access to specific pages.

features.title: Cross-platform availability
features.description: Available on web, Windows, macOS, iOS, and Android with real-time sync across devices.

pros: Extremely flexible block-and-database model adapts to many different workflows
Can replace several single-purpose tools (wiki, docs, task tracker) in one workspace
Large ecosystem of templates and community resources
Increasingly capable AI features for summarization, search, and multi-step tasks
Generous free tier for individuals and students
cons: Full Notion AI functionality is now limited to Business and Enterprise plans
Flexibility means more setup and configuration effort than opinionated tools
Performance can slow down in very large, deeply nested workspaces
Free plan caps file uploads and page history, limiting solo use over time
Enterprise pricing requires a custom sales conversation rather than transparent list pricing

faqs.question: Is Notion free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Notion offers a free plan for individuals with unlimited blocks, though it caps guest invites, file upload size, and page history.

faqs.question: How much does Notion cost for teams?
faqs.answer: Notion Plus is $10 per user per month billed annually, and Notion Business is roughly $20 per user per month billed annually; Enterprise pricing is custom.

faqs.question: Does Notion include AI features?
faqs.answer: Yes, but full Notion AI access, including the Notion Agent and AI meeting notes, is now bundled exclusively into the Business and Enterprise plans.

faqs.question: Who founded Notion?
faqs.answer: Notion was founded in 2013 by Ivan Zhao and Simon Last and is headquartered in San Francisco, California.

faqs.question: What is Notion's valuation?
faqs.answer: Notion reached an $11 billion valuation following a secondary tender offer in January 2026.

faqs.question: What can Notion be used for?
faqs.answer: Notion is commonly used for note-taking, team wikis, project and task management, documentation, and databases, often replacing several separate tools.

faqs.question: What are the main alternatives to Notion?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Confluence, Coda, ClickUp, Airtable, and Obsidian, depending on whether the primary use case is documentation, project management, or personal notes.

use_cases.title: Team knowledge base and wiki
use_cases.description: Companies build a searchable internal wiki for documentation, onboarding, and process guides accessible to the whole team.

use_cases.title: Lightweight project management
use_cases.description: Teams track tasks and projects using Notion's board and timeline database views instead of a dedicated project management tool.
use_cases.audience: Small to mid-sized product and marketing teams

use_cases.title: Personal notes and planning
use_cases.description: Individuals use the free plan to organize personal notes, reading lists, and daily planning in one flexible workspace.

enrichment.short_description: Novo is a fintech platform offering free business checking accounts for small businesses, freelancers, and entrepreneurs, with no monthly fees and built-in tools like Novo Boost and Novo Reserves.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (no monthly fees on the core checking account)
enrichment.headquarters: New York, New York, United States
enrichment.seo_title: Novo Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Novo review 2026: free business checking account features, fees, Novo Boost, and how it compares to Mercury, Bluevine, and traditional business banks.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Novo is a financial technology company founded in 2016 by Michael Rangel, Tyler McIntyre, and Rijk Plasman, headquartered in New York City, that offers business checking accounts for small businesses, freelancers, entrepreneurs, and startups. Novo is not a bank itself; it is a fintech whose deposit accounts are provided through partner banks that are FDIC-insured, a common structure among modern neobanks. The company has raised approximately $298-337 million in total funding across multiple rounds, including a $40.7 million Series A and a $90 million Series B, with its most recent disclosed round being a Series C in August 2023, and it operates with roughly 400 employees as of 2026. Novo's core product is a genuinely free business checking account: no monthly maintenance fees, no minimum balance requirements, no fees on incoming or outgoing ACH transfers, no insufficient funds fees, and free mobile check deposits, physical bank checks, and debit card replacements. It also refunds up to $7 per month in third-party ATM fees and allows fee-free withdrawals at any ATM worldwide. Rather than charging subscription tiers like some competitors, Novo monetizes primarily through interchange revenue on debit card transactions and value-added features. Key product features include Novo Boost, which accelerates payout speed for businesses using Stripe and other integrated payment processors; Novo Reserves, which let users create up to 10 sub-accounts to automatically set aside funds for taxes, payroll, or savings goals; and integrations with accounting and e-commerce tools like QuickBooks, Xero, Shopify, and Stripe. Novo's target audience is solo entrepreneurs, freelancers, and small business owners, particularly those running e-commerce or service-based businesses who want a modern, app-first banking experience without the fee structures and paperwork typical of traditional business bank accounts. Its primary competitors include Mercury, Bluevine, Relay, and Found, as well as traditional small business banking products from Chase and Bank of America. Novo's key differentiator is its combination of a completely free core account with deep integrations into the software small businesses already use for payments and accounting, positioning it as a banking layer built specifically around modern small-business workflows rather than a retrofit of traditional retail banking.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Novo was founded in 2016 by Michael Rangel, Tyler McIntyre, and Rijk Plasman and is headquartered in New York City. It provides free business checking accounts for small businesses, freelancers, and startups through FDIC-insured partner banks, operating as a fintech rather than a chartered bank itself.

The company has raised close to $300 million across multiple funding rounds through a Series C in 2023, and has grown to roughly 400 employees while focusing on serving solo entrepreneurs and small businesses that traditional banks often underserve.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Novo's core checking account carries no monthly fees, no minimum balance requirement, and no charges for incoming or outgoing ACH transfers, insufficient funds, or debit card replacement. It also refunds third-party ATM fees up to $7 per month and allows free withdrawals at any ATM worldwide.

Novo Boost speeds up access to funds from payment processors like Stripe, while Novo Reserves let business owners create up to 10 sub-accounts to automatically set aside money for taxes, payroll, or savings goals. Deep integrations with QuickBooks, Xero, Shopify, and Stripe streamline bookkeeping and payment workflows.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Novo's core business checking account is entirely free, with no monthly maintenance fees or minimum balance requirements. The company generates revenue primarily through debit card interchange fees rather than charging users directly for the account itself.
Certain services, such as outgoing domestic wire transfers (typically $25-$30) and expedited card shipping, carry a fee, but the vast majority of everyday banking activity remains free.

features.title: Fee-free business checking
features.description: No monthly fees, no minimum balance requirements, and no charges for incoming or outgoing ACH transfers.

features.title: Novo Boost
features.description: Speeds up access to funds from integrated payment processors such as Stripe, reducing payout wait times.

features.title: Novo Reserves
features.description: Lets business owners create up to 10 sub-accounts to automatically set aside funds for taxes, payroll, or savings goals.

features.title: Fee-free ATM access
features.description: Allows withdrawals at any ATM worldwide and refunds up to $7 per month in third-party ATM fees.

features.title: Accounting and e-commerce integrations
features.description: Connects with QuickBooks, Xero, Shopify, and Stripe to streamline bookkeeping and payment reconciliation.

features.title: Free invoicing and bill pay
features.description: Includes free mobile check deposit, free bill pay with physical checks, and free bank checks.

features.title: Mobile-first account management
features.description: A mobile app lets business owners manage transactions, reserves, and account settings on the go.

pros: Genuinely free checking account with no monthly maintenance fees
Novo Reserves make it easy to set aside funds for taxes or savings without a separate account
Strong integrations with Stripe, Shopify, QuickBooks, and Xero for small business workflows
No fees for ACH transfers, insufficient funds, or debit card replacement
Modern, app-first user experience compared to traditional business banks
cons: Not a bank itself, deposits are held through a partner bank rather than Novo directly
No interest-bearing checking or built-in high-yield savings option
Limited to a single core account type, less product breadth than some competitors like Mercury
Outgoing domestic wire transfers carry a fee
Customer support and dispute resolution can be slower than at larger traditional banks

faqs.question: Is Novo a bank?
faqs.answer: No, Novo is a fintech company, not a chartered bank; its checking accounts are provided through FDIC-insured partner banks.

faqs.question: Does Novo charge monthly fees?
faqs.answer: No, Novo's core business checking account has no monthly maintenance fees, no minimum balance requirements, and no ACH transfer fees.

faqs.question: What is Novo Boost?
faqs.answer: Novo Boost is a feature that speeds up access to funds from payment processors like Stripe, reducing payout wait times for businesses.

faqs.question: What are Novo Reserves?
faqs.answer: Novo Reserves let business owners create up to 10 sub-accounts within their Novo account to automatically set aside money for taxes, payroll, or other savings goals.

faqs.question: When was Novo founded?
faqs.answer: Novo was founded in 2016 by Michael Rangel, Tyler McIntyre, and Rijk Plasman and is headquartered in New York City.

faqs.question: Does Novo charge ATM fees?
faqs.answer: Novo allows free withdrawals at any ATM worldwide and refunds up to $7 per month in third-party ATM fees charged by the ATM operator.

faqs.question: What are the main alternatives to Novo?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Mercury, Bluevine, Relay, and Found, as well as small business accounts from traditional banks like Chase and Bank of America.

use_cases.title: Freelancer and solo entrepreneur banking
use_cases.description: Independent contractors and freelancers separate personal and business finances with a free, no-minimum checking account.

use_cases.title: E-commerce payout management
use_cases.description: Online sellers use Novo Boost to get faster access to Stripe and Shopify payouts and track cash flow in real time.

use_cases.title: Small business budgeting with sub-accounts
use_cases.description: Small business owners use Novo Reserves to automatically set aside funds for taxes, payroll, or growth goals without opening separate bank accounts.
